focus_ar: ربط المتحرك بالمتحرك — إدخال أصوات انتقالية /w/ أو /j/
arabic_challenge: When a word ending in a vowel sound is followed by a word starting with a vowel sound, Arabic often uses a glottal stop or brief pause between them (much like starting a new word cleanly with a hamza). English instead glides smoothly from one vowel into the next using a tiny inserted /w/ or /j/ sound, with no pause or throat closure at all.

isolation.instructions: When the FIRST word ends in a rounded vowel sound (like /oʊ/ in 'go' or /uː/ in 'you'), a tiny /w/ glide naturally appears to connect it to the next word's vowel.
isolation.instructions_ar: لما الكلمة الأولى تنتهي بصوت متحرك مدور الشفاه (زي /oʊ/ في go أو /uː/ في you)، بيظهر صوت /w/ صغير تلقائيًا يوصلها بالحرف المتحرك في الكلمة اللي بعدها.

sentence_practice: Let's go out — who is coming with us?
Do it now, it's too easy to wait.
Go away — I have blue eyes, not you.
daily_drills.record_this: Let's go out — who is coming? Do it now, it's too easy, so go away and let me finish with my blue eyes on the clock.
daily_drills.self_check: Say 'go out' fast — do you feel a tiny /w/ sound sneaking in between the two words, almost like 'go-wout'?

isolation.instructions: When the FIRST word ends in a spread, front vowel sound (like /iː/ in 'she' or /eɪ/ in 'day'), a tiny /j/ glide (like the 'y' in 'yes') naturally appears to connect it to the next vowel.
isolation.instructions_ar: لما الكلمة الأولى تنتهي بصوت متحرك أمامي ممدود (زي /iː/ في she أو /eɪ/ في day)، بيظهر صوت /j/ صغير (زي y في yes) يوصلها بالحرف المتحرك اللي بعده.

sentence_practice: She asked if I am taking a day off.
We are going to the office with my aunt.
I am not sure she asked the right question.
daily_drills.record_this: She asked if I am taking a day off, but we are going to the office with my aunt anyway.
daily_drills.self_check: Say 'I am' fast — do you hear a light /j/ sound connecting them, almost like 'I-yam'?

target_sounds: contrast: /w/ glide vs. /j/ glide, choosing correctly

isolation.instructions: The glide you insert depends ENTIRELY on the vowel sound at the end of the FIRST word — rounded vowels (oo, oh) pull in /w/; spread vowels (ee, ay) pull in /j/. Today, practice choosing correctly.
isolation.instructions_ar: الصوت الانتقالي اللي هتضيفه بيعتمد كليًا على صوت المتحرك في نهاية الكلمة الأولى — المتحركات المدورة بتسحب /w/، والمتحركات الممدودة بتسحب /j/.

sentence_practice: She asked if we are going out today.
Who is taking a day off? I am!
It's too easy — go out and see for yourself.
daily_drills.record_this: She asked who is going out today — I am taking a day off, and it's too easy to just go out and enjoy my blue eyes in the sun.
daily_drills.self_check: For each linked pair in this sentence, could you correctly identify whether it needed a /w/ or a /j/ glide before saying it?

target_sounds: vowel-vowel linking across a full phrase

isolation.instructions: Combine multiple vowel-to-vowel links in a row to build fluency in longer stretches of connected speech.
isolation.instructions_ar: دمج أكتر من ربط متحرك-بمتحرك ورا بعض لبناء انسيابية في مقاطع أطول من الكلام المتصل.
practice: Go away, I am busy → go-WAway, I-YAM busy
She asked who is here → she-YASKED who-WIZ here

pair: go away, I am busy (fully glided)

sentence_practice: Go away, I am busy right now.
She asked who is here at the office.
It's too old, and I am too easy to please anyway.
daily_drills.record_this: Go away, I am busy — she asked who is here, but it's too old a question, and I am too easy to please anyway.
daily_drills.self_check: In this sentence, count every vowel-to-vowel link — did you correctly glide each one without pausing?

target_sounds: vowel-vowel linking at natural conversational speed

isolation.instructions: As with the consonant linking from Week 3, this only sounds natural at real conversational speed — practice building speed without losing the glide.
isolation.instructions_ar: زي ربط الساكن من الأسبوع ٣، ده بيبان طبيعي بس في سرعة كلام حقيقية — تدرب على زيادة السرعة بدون خسارة صوت الانتقال.

sentence_practice: Just go out already — who is stopping you?
I am too easy to please, she asked me today.
We are taking a day off, and it's too easy to enjoy.
daily_drills.record_this: Just go out already — who is stopping you? I am too easy to please, and we are taking a day off because it's too easy to enjoy.
daily_drills.self_check: Record this 3 times, increasing speed. Did your /w/ and /j/ glides survive at full conversational speed?

daily_drills.target_sounds: Review: /w/ glide, /j/ glide, choosing correctly, phrase-level linking, natural speed
challenge_sentences: She asked who is going out today, and I am too easy to say no.
Go away — we are taking a day off, and it's too easy to enjoy the blue eyes of the sky.
Who is here? I am! Go out and see for yourself, it's too easy.
daily_drills.record_this: She asked who is going out today. I am too easy to say no — go away, we are taking a day off, and it's too easy to enjoy the blue sky.
daily_drills.self_check: Mark every vowel-to-vowel link in this passage as either /w/ or /j/. Did you use each type correctly and consistently?

test_yourself.instructions: Record this passage naturally, paying attention to /w/ and /j/ glides between vowel-ending and vowel-starting words.
test_yourself.passage: She asked if I am free to go out today, and I am! Who is coming with us? We are meeting my aunt at the office first, then we'll go away to the lake — it's too easy to say no to a day off like this. The blue sky and blue eyes of summer make everything feel new again.

sounds_to_check./w/ glide (rounded vowel endings): go out, who is, too easy, go away, blue eyes
sounds_to_check./j/ glide (spread vowel endings): she asked, I am (x2), day off, the office, my aunt

scoring_guide.90_100: Correct glide (w or j) consistently chosen and inserted naturally at conversational speed throughout the whole passage.
scoring_guide.70_89: Mostly correct; occasional wrong glide choice or a missed link under pressure.
scoring_guide.50_69: Glides present in drilled words only, inconsistent in new combinations.
scoring_guide.below_50: Glottal-stop/pause style delivery between vowels throughout — go back and repeat Days 1-4.

for_arabic_speakers: This is blueprint Rule 2 of connected speech, building directly on Week 3's Rule 1 (consonant-to-vowel linking).

The glide is NOT something you consciously insert like an extra sound — it emerges naturally from your mouth's shape as it moves from one vowel to the next. Focus on NOT pausing, and the glide often appears automatically.
If you catch yourself inserting a glottal stop /ʔ/ instead of a glide, that's the Arabic habit surfacing — consciously relax your throat and let the vowels flow into each other.
supplementary_notes.recording_requirement: Record the 'record_this' sentence every day. By Day 7, listen for zero glottal stops between vowel-vowel word boundaries.
